ecology.comments: Inhabits warm, flowing streams. Prefers rocky crevices.
ecology.biotope: Central American rivers and streams with moderate current and rocky or gravel substrates.
ecology.socialBehavior: Must be kept in a conspecific school of 5+ individuals to manage natural cichlid pecking orders.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Requires high-quality flakes/pellets as a daily staple. Supplement with live or frozen foods such as bloodworms, brine shrimp, and daphnia to copy their natural riverine diets.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide flat slate rocks, clay pots, or breeding caves. They lay eggs on these flat surfaces and defend them aggressively as a breeding pair.
reproduction.comments: Highly protective parents. Will guard the eggs and fry against all tankmates, making separate hatchery setups recommended.

vibeLine.casual: The tiny tank tyrant that breeds at the drop of a hat.
vibeLine.pro: Amatitlania nigrofasciata. Hardy, pugnacious cichlid; breeds relentlessly, guards fiercely.

flavorText.casual: Small but absolutely fearless, they'll boss around fish triple their size. Pair two up and you'll have babies almost immediately. Hardy and fascinating, just don't expect them to share the tank politely.
flavorText.pro: Amatitlania nigrofasciata is a hardy Central American cichlid and a textbook beginner breeder, pairing off and guarding fry with relentless aggression. Keep groups of five-plus to spread tension, or a single pair in a species tank.

ecology.comments: Inhabits fast-flowing streams and rivers. Grazes on driftwood.
ecology.biotope: Fast-flowing, highly oxygenated tributaries of the Amazon basin, typically with stony beds and sunken wood.
ecology.socialBehavior: Generally peaceful, but males are territorial over hiding spots and caves.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Provide sinking algae wafers, fresh zucchini, spinach, and cucumber. Driftwood MUST be present in the tank as they need to ingest cellulose for proper digestion.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide narrow breeding caves, ceramic tubes, or hollow logs.
reproduction.comments: The male attracts the female to a cave, where she lays eggs. The male then guards and fans the eggs aggressively until they hatch.

vibeLine.casual: The bristly little algae machine that stays small.
vibeLine.pro: Ancistrus cirrhosus. Compact algae-grazer; needs driftwood for proper digestion.

flavorText.casual: The pleco that won't outgrow your tank, with a face only an aquarist could love. They graze algae tirelessly and need driftwood to rasp on. Far more practical than those foot-long common plecos.
flavorText.pro: Ancistrus cirrhosus stays compact and grazes algae, but requires driftwood to ingest cellulose for digestion. A cave-brooder; males grow bristles and guard eggs in a tube. Males are territorial over caves, so provide several.

vibeLine.casual: The little king that demands his own palace.
vibeLine.pro: Betta splendens. Territorial and requires warm, stable water with gentle surface access.

flavorText.casual: Gorgeous and full of personality. They like having their space and will flare at their own reflection sometimes.
flavorText.pro: Best kept alone or with carefully chosen tank mates. Requires warm water (24–28°C) and access to the surface for breathing. Avoid strong currents.

hiddenLore: Magikarp from Pokémon. The ultimate underdog fish. Splash around long enough with perfect water parameters and you too might evolve into something legendary ✨
evolutionLore: Congratulations! Your Magikarp has evolved into Gyarados! The most powerful and intimidating fish in the tank. Rawr! 🐲

vibeLine.casual: The original tank buster that refuses to stay small.
vibeLine.pro: Carassius auratus. Coldwater species that grows much larger than most people expect.

flavorText.casual: Cute when small. Gets massive. Needs serious space and strong filtration. Not actually a beginner fish despite what the pet store said.
flavorText.pro: Common goldfish can live for decades and grow over 12 inches. Requires large tanks or ponds with excellent filtration. Not suitable for small aquariums long-term.

vibeLine.casual: The cheerful armored catfish that scoots in groups.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras aeneus. Hardy, peaceful bottom-dweller; needs a group and sand.

flavorText.casual: Endlessly busy little bottom-dwellers that bumble around in groups looking for food. Hardy, peaceful, and full of charm, they're a brilliant first catfish. Give them soft sand and at least a half-dozen friends.
flavorText.pro: Corydoras aeneus is a hardy, peaceful schooling catfish best kept in groups of six or more over smooth sand to protect their barbels. Egg-depositing spawners triggered by cool water changes. Avoid sharp substrate at all costs.

vibeLine.casual: The hardworking cleaner that actually eats the tough algae.
vibeLine.pro: Crossocheilus oblongus. Genuine algae-grazer; one of few that eats hair algae.

flavorText.casual: One of the few fish that genuinely tackles stubborn algae, including the dreaded black beard kind. Active and mostly peaceful, they get more boisterous with age. Make sure you buy the real thing, as look-alikes abound.
flavorText.pro: Crossocheilus oblongus is among the few fish that eat hair and black beard algae, though appetite shifts toward prepared foods as they mature. Active and semi-territorial when older. Frequently mislabeled; verify the species before buying.

ecology.comments: Found in slow-moving streams and rice paddies.
ecology.biotope: Shallow, slow-moving streams, rice paddies, and canals in South Asia (India and Bangladesh).
ecology.socialBehavior: Must be kept in a school of 6+ to allow active shoaling and reduce stress.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Thrives on high-quality flake foods, freeze-dried bloodworms, and live daphnia. Very active surface-feeders.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Fine gravel, glass marbles substrate, or a spawning grid to prevent adults from eating the dropped eggs.
reproduction.comments: Non-adhesive eggs are scattered over the substrate. Fast and prolific spawners. Remove parent fish immediately to ensure high yield.

vibeLine.casual: The tireless little speedster that never sits still.
vibeLine.pro: Danio rerio. Bulletproof, fast-moving shoaler ideal for cycling new tanks.

flavorText.casual: Hardy, energetic, and almost impossible to discourage. They zip around the top half of the tank all day and shrug off a wide range of temperatures. A great first fish, just give them room to sprint.
flavorText.pro: Danio rerio tolerates cooler water and wide parameter swings, making it a classic starter species. Keep six or more to curb fin-nipping energy. A tight-fitting lid is recommended, as they jump.

vibeLine.casual: The flowy-finned classic that fades from black to silver.
vibeLine.pro: Gymnocorymbus ternetzi. Hardy mid-water tetra; occasional fin-nipper in small groups.

flavorText.casual: An old-school favorite that's tough and adaptable. Their dusky fins fade with age but they keep their charm. Keep a proper group or they may get nippy with flowing fins.
flavorText.pro: Gymnocorymbus ternetzi is a robust, long-lived tetra. Understocked groups can nip trailing fins, so keep six or more. Tolerant of a broad parameter range, making it forgiving for newer keepers.

vibeLine.casual: The gentle one with a glowing orange racing stripe.
vibeLine.pro: Hemigrammus erythrozonus. Peaceful soft-water tetra for calm community tanks.

flavorText.casual: A quiet charmer with a neon-orange line that seems to glow under good lighting. Peaceful and easygoing, they pair well with other gentle fish. Give them a planted tank and a group to feel secure.
flavorText.pro: Hemigrammus erythrozonus is a peaceful, undemanding tetra that suits planted community setups. The luminous lateral stripe pops over dark substrate. Keep six or more for natural shoaling and reduced timidity.

ecology.comments: Prefers clean, clear or blackwater streams with dense vegetation.
ecology.biotope: Shaded blackwater streams and tributaries in the western Amazon basin, rich in tannins and leaf litter.
ecology.socialBehavior: Must be kept in a schooling group of 6+ (ideally 10+) individuals to reduce anxiety and display natural behavior.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Eats crushed micro-flakes, baby brine shrimp, and daphnia. Provide tiny food sizes due to their small mouth size.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Dense fine-leaved plants, java moss, or spawning mops under extremely dim lighting.
reproduction.comments: Eggs are highly light-sensitive and are scattered among fine vegetation. Parents should be removed immediately after spawning to prevent egg-eating.

vibeLine.casual: The classic little blue-and-red rocket that makes every tank feel alive.
vibeLine.pro: Paracheirodon innesi. Schooling tetra; best color in stable, soft water.

flavorText.casual: Tiny, fast, and ridiculously pretty. They look even better when they’re in a proper school. One or two just looks lonely.
flavorText.pro: Neon Tetras thrive in established, well-maintained tanks. Sensitive to ammonia and sudden parameter swings. Best kept in groups with gentle flow.

hiddenLore: Nami, the Tidecaller from League of Legends, secretly blesses this species. Keep your tank parameters perfect and she may grant you the perfect water flow ✨

vibeLine.casual: The hardy charmer happy in a cooler tank.
vibeLine.pro: Pethia conchonius. Robust, active barb tolerant of unheated coolwater setups.

flavorText.casual: Tough, active, and forgiving of beginner mistakes. Males flush rosy-pink, especially in cooler water. They're energetic swimmers, so give them space and a few friends to chase around.
flavorText.pro: Pethia conchonius is a robust, active barb comfortable in unheated tanks down to roughly 18°C. Keep six or more to spread out their energetic, occasionally nippy behavior. Males color up best in cooler water.

ecology.comments: Highly adaptable. Found in wide range of fresh and brackish waters.
ecology.biotope: Various habitats including clear streams, canals, and brackish coastal waters of northern South America.
ecology.socialBehavior: Keep in a ratio of 1 male to 2+ females to prevent excessive harassment of females.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Enjoys spirulina-enriched flakes, freeze-dried daphnia, and micro-pellets. Needs a balance of vegetable and protein matter.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide floating plants (like Hornwort, Water Wisteria) or fry traps.
reproduction.comments: Gives birth to free-swimming live fry rather than laying eggs. Fry hide in floating vegetation to avoid predation by adults.

vibeLine.casual: The colorful, unstoppable little breeders of the hobby.
vibeLine.pro: Poecilia reticulata. Extremely hardy and prolific livebearer.

flavorText.casual: Beautiful, easy to keep, and they multiply faster than you expect. Great for beginners, but be ready for babies.
flavorText.pro: One of the hardiest and most adaptable species. Excellent for new tanks and community setups, though selective breeding lines can be more delicate.

vibeLine.casual: The easygoing livebearer that loves hard, mineral-rich water.
vibeLine.pro: Poecilia sphenops. Hard-water livebearer; prolific and mildly brackish-tolerant.

flavorText.casual: Friendly, active, and quick to breed, so you'll have fry before you know it. They do best in harder, alkaline water with some mineral content. Give them space; they're bigger and busier than they look.
flavorText.pro: Poecilia sphenops thrives in hard, alkaline water and tolerates low-end brackish conditions. A prolific livebearer that drops broods every four to six weeks; provide cover or separate gravid females to save fry. Soft, acidic water invites health issues.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide flat vertical surfaces such as broad leaves (Amazon Sword), slate, or vertical slate/cones for egg deposition.
reproduction.comments: A devoted pair cleans a vertical surface and guards the eggs and fry together.

vibeLine.casual: The tall, graceful icon of the planted tank.
vibeLine.pro: Pterophyllum scalare. Tall cichlid; peaceful until paired, then territorial.

flavorText.casual: Elegant, tall, and full of presence, they glide through a planted tank like living sails. They're cichlids, though, so they get bossy when breeding. Don't trust them around tiny fish like neons.
flavorText.pro: Pterophyllum scalare is a tall-bodied cichlid, peaceful in groups until a pair forms and claims territory. Bonded pairs are devoted vertical-surface spawners, laying eggs on broad leaves or slate. Needs tank height; will eat fish small enough to fit its mouth.

vibeLine.casual: The cheeky stripe gang that nips first and asks questions never.
vibeLine.pro: Puntigrus tetrazona. Active semi-aggressive barb; keep in groups of six-plus.

flavorText.casual: Bold, fast, and full of attitude. Keep them in a proper group of six or more and they'll squabble among themselves instead of harassing your other fish. Skip the long-finned tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Puntigrus tetrazona is a notorious fin-nipper when understocked. A tight group of eight or more diffuses aggression within the shoal. Avoid pairing with bettas, angelfish, or other slow, trailing-finned species.

vibeLine.casual: The mellow barb that blushes deep cherry red.
vibeLine.pro: Puntius titteya. Peaceful, hardy barb; males redden during display.

flavorText.casual: Unlike its pushier barb cousins, this one is calm and easygoing. Males turn a rich cherry red when they're showing off. Hardy and adaptable, they're a relaxed pick for a community tank.
flavorText.pro: Puntius titteya is a peaceful exception among barbs. Males intensify to deep red during courtship and competition. Hardy and adaptable; a small group keeps them confident and colorful.

vibeLine.casual: The shy golden sweetheart that warms up to you.
vibeLine.pro: Trichogaster chuna. Peaceful labyrinth fish; far hardier than the dwarf gourami.

flavorText.casual: A gentle little golden glow of a fish. They're timid at first but grow bold and curious once settled. Calm, undemanding, and a much hardier choice than the flashier dwarf gourami.
flavorText.pro: Trichogaster chuna is a peaceful labyrinth fish that breathes surface air and prefers calm, planted water. Hardier and less disease-prone than Trichogaster lalius. Males build bubblenests and color up deep amber when breeding.

vibeLine.casual: The shimmering centerpiece that breathes air from the surface.
vibeLine.pro: Trichogaster lalius. Labyrinth fish; watch for Dwarf Gourami Iridovirus in stock.

flavorText.casual: Males are living jewels in shades of red and blue, and they gulp air from the surface like little dragons. They can be a bit shy, so give them calm tankmates and gentle flow. Choose healthy stock carefully.
flavorText.pro: Trichogaster lalius is a labyrinth fish that breathes atmospheric air and prefers calm, warm water. Source carefully, as farmed stock is prone to Dwarf Gourami Iridovirus and bacterial infections. One male per tank avoids conflict.

vibeLine.casual: The peaceful one wearing a little black triangle.
vibeLine.pro: Trigonostigma heteromorpha. Hardy, peaceful shoaler; signature black wedge marking.

flavorText.casual: A longtime community favorite with a coppery body and a bold black wedge. Calm, hardy, and happy in a group, they're a great match for almost any peaceful tank. They love planted setups.
flavorText.pro: Trigonostigma heteromorpha is a hardy, peaceful staple with its distinctive black triangle. Keeps best in groups of eight or more in soft to neutral water. Reliable and undemanding in planted community tanks.

ecology.comments: Prefers fast-flowing streams and rivers with vegetation.
ecology.biotope: Fast-flowing rivers and streams with rocky bottom substrates and bankside vegetation in Central America.
ecology.socialBehavior: Avoid keeping multiple males in small tanks as they establish strict hierarchies and fight.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Eats flake food, veggie wafers, and frozen brine shrimp. Appreciates regular vegetable matter supplements.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Dense floating plant coverage or breeding box to protect newborn fry.
reproduction.comments: Internal fertilization occurs. Females give birth to large batches of live young. Very prolific breeders in well-planted tanks.

vibeLine.casual: The livebearer with a built-in sword tail.
vibeLine.pro: Xiphophorus hellerii. Active hard-water livebearer; males spar and may jump.

flavorText.casual: Named for the long sword trailing off the male's tail. They're active, hardy, and breed readily in harder water. Keep one male with several females and use a lid, as they're known jumpers.
flavorText.pro: Xiphophorus hellerii is a hardy, active livebearer for harder water. Breeds readily; keep one male to two or three females to spread harassment and protect fry with dense planting. Multiple males spar, and they are strong jumpers, so keep the tank covered.

vibeLine.casual: The cheerful, no-fuss splash of color for beginners.
vibeLine.pro: Xiphophorus maculatus. Hardy, peaceful livebearer in endless color strains.

flavorText.casual: One of the friendliest fish you can buy, in just about every color imaginable. Peaceful, hardy, and happy to breed, they're a classic first livebearer. Harder water keeps them at their best.
flavorText.pro: Xiphophorus maculatus is a forgiving, peaceful livebearer available in countless line-bred colors. Prefers harder, alkaline water. Breeds readily, so expect fry in any mixed-sex group.

vibeLine.casual: The hardy platy that shrugs off cooler water.
vibeLine.pro: Xiphophorus variatus. Cold-tolerant, hardy platy with broad temperature range.

flavorText.casual: A close cousin of the southern platy, but even tougher and fine in cooler tanks. Just as colorful and just as easy. A great choice if your room runs cold and you'd rather skip a heater.
flavorText.pro: Xiphophorus variatus is a hardy platy tolerant of cooler temperatures, down toward 18°C. Equally colorful and prolific as its cousin. Suits unheated or temperate community setups.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide a large flat rock or smooth slate; pairs clean a flat horizontal surface for egg deposition.
reproduction.comments: Devoted parents; both guard the eggs and fry vigorously and may move them to pits.

vibeLine.casual: The big wet puppy that rearranges its tank.
vibeLine.pro: Astronotus ocellatus. Large, intelligent cichlid; heavy bioload and big footprint.

flavorText.casual: Smart, messy, and full of personality, they learn to recognize you and beg for food like a dog. They also redecorate constantly and grow huge. A real commitment, but endlessly entertaining.
flavorText.pro: Astronotus ocellatus is an intelligent cichlid that reaches a foot long and produces a heavy bioload, demanding a large tank and serious filtration. Pairs are devoted substrate spawners. Will swallow any tankmate that fits in its mouth.

vibeLine.casual: The silver "shark" that's really a gentle giant.
vibeLine.pro: Balantiocheilos melanopterus. Large, skittish schooling cyprinid; needs serious swimming space.

flavorText.casual: They look like sleek little sharks but they're peaceful, jumpy cyprinids that grow surprisingly large. They need a big tank, a group, and a tight lid. Stunning in a spacious setup, cramped in a small one.
flavorText.pro: Balantiocheilos melanopterus is a peaceful but skittish schooling cyprinid that reaches roughly a foot and needs a long, large tank with a group. Strong jumpers, so cover the tank. Not a true shark despite the profile.

vibeLine.casual: The shiny giant barb that schools and grows fast.
vibeLine.pro: Barbonymus schwanenfeldii. Large active schooling barb; needs a sizable tank.

flavorText.casual: Big, fast, and gleaming silver, they school energetically and grow far larger than the barbs you find in nano tanks. They need real swimming room and a group. Impressive in a large setup, cramped anywhere small.
flavorText.pro: Barbonymus schwanenfeldii is a large, active schooling barb that reaches over a foot and demands a big tank with strong filtration. Keep five or more to reduce skittishness. May nibble soft plants; rarely bred at home.

vibeLine.casual: The playful orange clown that needs lots of friends.
vibeLine.pro: Chromobotia macracanthus. Large, social loach; grows far beyond most expectations.

flavorText.casual: Striped orange goofballs that play, hide, and even lie on their sides like they've fainted. They're social and need a group to thrive. Just know they get big and live for decades, so plan ahead.
flavorText.pro: Chromobotia macracanthus is a social loach that needs groups of five-plus and eventually a large tank, reaching well over a foot in time. Rarely bred in home aquaria. Sensitive to ich and poor water; acclimate carefully.

vibeLine.casual: The little pandas with the black eye patches.
vibeLine.pro: Corydoras panda. Peaceful bottom dweller that prefers cooler, well-oxygenated water.

flavorText.casual: Adorable little armored catfish that love to hang out in groups on the bottom. Very peaceful and fun to watch.
flavorText.pro: Best kept in groups of 5+. They appreciate cooler temperatures and sandy substrate. Sensitive to poor water quality and strong medications.

vibeLine.casual: The jet-black "shark" with one brilliant red tail.
vibeLine.pro: Epalzeorhynchos bicolor. Bold, territorial cyprinid; striking but scrappy with others.

flavorText.casual: Velvet-black with a single vivid red tail, it's a real eye-catcher with a feisty streak. It guards its turf and chases intruders. Keep just one, with tankmates fast enough to stay out of trouble.
flavorText.pro: Epalzeorhynchos bicolor is a bold, territorial cyprinid, best kept one per tank with plenty of cover to break sightlines. Aggressive toward similar bottom species. A near-threatened species in the wild; not a true shark.

vibeLine.casual: The black-bodied "shark" with bright red fins.
vibeLine.pro: Epalzeorhynchos frenatum. Territorial bottom cyprinid; aggressive toward its own kind.

flavorText.casual: A striking dark fish with fiery red fins and a bold attitude to match. They patrol the bottom and don't tolerate others of their kind. Best kept singly with fast tankmates that can shrug off the bossiness.
flavorText.pro: Epalzeorhynchos frenatum is a territorial bottom-dwelling cyprinid, aggressive to conspecifics and similar species. Keep one per tank with hiding spots and quick, mid-water company. Not a shark; the name is purely cosmetic.

vibeLine.casual: The big pink one famous for "kissing" contests.
vibeLine.pro: Helostoma temminckii. Large gourami; "kissing" is territorial sparring, not affection.

flavorText.casual: Famous for the lip-locking "kisses," which are actually little turf battles, not romance. They get surprisingly large and graze constantly. Give them room and they're a hardy, characterful centerpiece.
flavorText.pro: Helostoma temminckii grows large and uses its mouth to spar over territory rather than show affection. A constant grazer that needs volume and filtration. Unlike most gouramis, it scatters eggs at the surface and builds no nest.

ecology.comments: Found in slow-flowing rivers and ponds with rich vegetation.
ecology.biotope: Slow-flowing tributaries, oxbows, and swampy areas in the Amazon and Paraguay river basins.
ecology.socialBehavior: Must be kept in a school of 6+ to minimize fin-nipping behavior directed at slower tankmates.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Accepts high-quality flake foods and small pellets. Supplement with frozen bloodworms and brine shrimp to maintain color vibrancy.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Java moss beds, spawning grids, or fine plants. Eggs adhere to the plant leaves.
reproduction.comments: Spawns in pairs or small groups. Parents are notorious egg-eaters and must be separated after the spawn event completes.

vibeLine.casual: The fiery red scrapper with a taste for fins.
vibeLine.pro: Hyphessobrycon eques. Striking but nippy; needs large groups to settle.

flavorText.casual: Gorgeous deep-red color, but they've got a feisty streak. A big group keeps their fin-nipping focused on each other rather than your other fish. Avoid slow, long-finned tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Hyphessobrycon eques is a beautiful but notorious nipper. Groups of eight or more disperse aggression internally. Best paired with fast, robust species; never with bettas or angelfish.

vibeLine.casual: The stunning scrapper that thrives in cool water.
vibeLine.pro: Macropodus opercularis. Hardy but pugnacious labyrinth fish; tolerates cold water.

flavorText.casual: One of the oldest fish in the hobby, and gorgeous, but it has a temper. Tough enough to handle cool, unheated tanks. Beautiful kept alone or with fish too big to bully.
flavorText.pro: Macropodus opercularis is extremely hardy and cold-tolerant, but aggressive toward small or similar-shaped tankmates. Males are especially territorial and build bubblenests. Best as a centerpiece in a coolwater setup with robust company.

diet.fooditems: sifts substrate for plant material and small organisms

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide flat stones or broad leaves as spawning sites.
reproduction.comments: Spawns on flat surfaces; females fan the eggs until hatching and both parents care for the fry.

vibeLine.casual: The mellow dwarf cichlid that's easy to please.
vibeLine.pro: Mikrogeophagus altispinosus. Hardy dwarf cichlid; far more forgiving than German rams.

flavorText.casual: All the charm of a dwarf cichlid with far less drama. They're hardier and more tolerant than German blue rams, and they sift sand and shimmy at each other adorably. A great first cichlid.
flavorText.pro: Mikrogeophagus altispinosus is a hardy dwarf cichlid that tolerates a wider parameter range than Mikrogeophagus ramirezi. Bonded pairs are gentle substrate spawners. Provide sand for sifting and flat stones for spawning sites.

vibeLine.casual: The colorful little cichlid with main character energy.
vibeLine.pro: Mikrogeophagus ramirezi. Demands warm, soft, acidic water and pristine conditions.

flavorText.casual: Gorgeous but dramatic. They like their water warm and clean, and they will let you know if they’re not happy.
flavorText.pro: Sensitive to cool temperatures and poor water quality. Best kept in pairs in well-established tanks with soft, acidic parameters around pH 6.5 and 26–28°C.

vibeLine.casual: The little eel-like noodle that loves to burrow.
vibeLine.pro: Pangio kuhlii. Peaceful nocturnal loach; needs soft sand and groups.

flavorText.casual: Tiny banded noodles that wriggle through the substrate and pop out at feeding time. Shy and peaceful, they feel safest in a group with soft sand to burrow into. A fun, quirky bottom crew.
flavorText.pro: Pangio kuhlii is a peaceful, nocturnal loach that burrows, so soft sand and hiding spots are essential. Keep five or more to draw them out. Sensitive to medications and poor water; rarely bred in captivity.

vibeLine.casual: Like a neon tetra that went all-in on red.
vibeLine.pro: Paracheirodon axelrodi. Soft-water schooler with full-length red stripe.

flavorText.casual: Brighter and bolder than the neon, with red running the whole length of the body. They look stunning in a big group against dark substrate and plants. Happiest in soft, slightly acidic water.
flavorText.pro: Paracheirodon axelrodi shows its full red-and-blue brilliance in soft, acidic blackwater. Best in large shoals of ten or more over dark substrate. More sensitive to hard water than the common neon.

vibeLine.casual: The red-nosed schooler that moves like one fish.
vibeLine.pro: Petitella rhodostoma. Tight-schooling water-quality barometer; red nose tracks health.

flavorText.casual: Famous for schooling so tightly they look choreographed. Their bright red noses fade when water quality drops, so they double as a health gauge. They like clean, stable, soft water.
flavorText.pro: Petitella rhodostoma schools more tightly than almost any tetra and signals stress by losing nose color. Demands stable, clean, soft water, hence its intermediate rating. Stunning in groups of ten or more.

vibeLine.casual: The molly that shows off with a giant sail.
vibeLine.pro: Poecilia velifera. Large hard-water molly; males display towering dorsal fin.

flavorText.casual: The showstopper of the molly world, where mature males raise a huge sail-like fin to impress. They need room, hard alkaline water, and warmth to look their best. Not a fish for a small tank.
flavorText.pro: Poecilia velifera is a large molly whose males develop a spectacular sailfin in spacious, hard, alkaline water. Often benefits from added marine salt. Needs warmth and volume to fully develop finnage.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide flat, hard surfaces (slate, rocks, or broad leaves) for egg deposition.
reproduction.comments: Attentive parents; both guard the eggs and fry vigorously.

vibeLine.casual: The heavyweight brawler that glitters with blue spangles.
vibeLine.pro: Rocio octofasciata. Large, aggressive cichlid; stunning spangling, serious territorial streak.

flavorText.casual: Named after a famous boxer for good reason. Mature fish are covered in electric blue-green spangles that flash under good light. Tough and territorial, they're a bold pick that needs space and careful tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Rocio octofasciata develops dazzling blue-green spangling and an equally strong territorial temper, especially when breeding. A substrate spawner that guards fry hard. Needs a large tank and robust, similarly-sized company.

ecology.comments: Found in quiet acidic blackwater areas of the Amazon basin.
ecology.biotope: Slow-moving Amazonian tributaries and flooded forests under dense canopy cover.
ecology.socialBehavior: Must be kept in a conspecific group of 6+ individuals to prevent submissive stress and establish a natural social hierarchy.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Feed premium beefheart mixes, freeze-dried tubifex, and frozen bloodworms. Enjoys grazing on high-protein pellets throughout the day to support body growth.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide vertical slate, breeding cones, or broad-leafed plants (e.g., Amazon Swords) for vertical egg deposition.
reproduction.comments: Lays eggs on vertical surfaces. Parents produce a specialized skin mucus that serves as the primary food source for newly hatched fry.

vibeLine.casual: The round, regal "king" of the planted aquarium.
vibeLine.pro: Symphysodon aequifasciatus. Demanding warmwater cichlid; pristine, stable parameters essential.

flavorText.casual: Big, round, and stunning, they really do look like living dinner plates of color. They're also divas about water quality and warmth. A rewarding fish once you've got the basics down, not a starter pick.
flavorText.pro: Symphysodon aequifasciatus demands warm (28-30°C), soft, spotless water and a group of six or more to settle. Bonded pairs guard eggs and feed fry on body slime. Sensitive to nitrate and parameter swings; an advanced keeper's fish.

vibeLine.casual: The tough little gem that's happy in cooler water.
vibeLine.pro: Tanichthys albonubes. Subtropical shoaler; thrives unheated in cooler rooms.

flavorText.casual: Underrated and incredibly easy. They show their best colors in cooler water and don't even need a heater in a warm room. Peaceful, pretty, and perfect for a first tank.
flavorText.pro: Tanichthys albonubes is a hardy subtropical species best kept between 15 and 22°C. Colors intensify in groups and cooler conditions. An excellent unheated nano or coldwater community fish.

diet.fooditems: opportunistic omnivore — insects, plant matter, small invertebrates

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide flat stones or slate for egg deposition.
reproduction.comments: Highly developed brood care; both parents tend eggs and fry.

vibeLine.casual: The bluffer that flares a fiery red throat.
vibeLine.pro: Thorichthys meeki. Moderately peaceful cichlid; threat display over real aggression.

flavorText.casual: When they want to look tough, they puff out a brilliant red throat to seem bigger. Mostly bluff rather than brawl, they're one of the calmer cichlids. Great if you want personality without constant warfare.
flavorText.pro: Thorichthys meeki intimidates rivals by flaring its red throat and gill covers, relying on display more than damage. A relatively peaceful substrate-spawning cichlid that guards fry diligently. Tolerant tankmates and some cover keep things calm.

vibeLine.casual: The lace-patterned beauty that glows when it's happy.
vibeLine.pro: Trichopodus leerii. Peaceful, elegant gourami; needs warmth and height.

flavorText.casual: Covered in a delicate pearly net pattern, with breeding males flushing fiery orange on the throat. Peaceful and graceful, they're one of the easier large gouramis. Give them a calm tank with some height.
flavorText.pro: Trichopodus leerii is a peaceful, hardy gourami whose males develop a bright orange breast when spawning. A bubblenest builder that values warm, calm water and surface plants. One of the most forgiving mid-size labyrinth fish.

ecology.comments: Inhabits slow-moving streams and flooded areas.
ecology.biotope: Slow-moving streams, pools, and flooded grasslands in the Paraguay river basin.
ecology.socialBehavior: Best kept in pairs or harems (1 male to multiple females) to manage territorial aggression.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Provide small frozen food like daphnia, cyclops, and baby brine shrimp. Flakes are accepted but should not be the sole diet.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Provide half coconut shells, small clay caves, or dense leaf litter.
reproduction.comments: Maternal mouthbrooder / cave breeder. The female guards the eggs inside the cave while the male defends the outer territory.

vibeLine.casual: A speck of bright red that's somehow a whole fish.
vibeLine.pro: Boraras brigittae. Micro-shoaler for soft-water nano and shrimp tanks.

flavorText.casual: Tiny but unforgettable, with males lighting up deep red in a well-planted nano tank. They're shy at first and shine once settled in a confident group. Soft water and gentle tankmates suit them best.
flavorText.pro: Boraras brigittae is a true micro-fish for mature, soft-water nano tanks. Color deepens in large groups over dark substrate. Too small for boisterous tankmates; pairs ideally with dwarf shrimp.

vibeLine.casual: Tiny galaxies swimming in your tank.
vibeLine.pro: Danio margaritatus. Peaceful schooling fish that prefers cooler, well-oxygenated water.

flavorText.casual: These little guys look like they have stars on their bodies. Super peaceful and love swimming in groups.
flavorText.pro: Best kept in schools of 6+. They prefer slightly cooler temperatures (22–24°C) and appreciate gentle flow with plenty of swimming space.

vibeLine.casual: A tiny glowing ember drifting through your plants.
vibeLine.pro: Hyphessobrycon amandae. Peaceful nano tetra; warm orange best in groups.

flavorText.casual: Little flecks of warm orange that look amazing in a planted nano tank. They're peaceful and shoal tightly when they feel safe. Perfect with shrimp and other small, gentle tankmates.
flavorText.pro: Hyphessobrycon amandae is an ideal nano species, glowing orange against greenery. Keep ten or more for confident shoaling and best color. Peaceful enough to cohabit with dwarf shrimp.

vibeLine.casual: A pocket-sized firework crossed with a guppy.
vibeLine.pro: Poecilia wingei. Tiny, vivid livebearer; hybridizes freely with guppies.

flavorText.casual: Smaller than a guppy but somehow even flashier, with males that look like walking neon paint jobs. They're hardy, lively, and breed constantly. Keep them away from guppies if you want pure Endlers.
flavorText.pro: Poecilia wingei is a small, intensely colored livebearer for hard water. It interbreeds readily with guppies, so isolate to preserve pure strains. Prolific and active in groups.

ecology.comments: Native to clearwater creeks and heavily vegetated margins of the lower Ucayali basin in Peru. Highly gregarious and social.
ecology.biotope: Clearwater creeks and heavily vegetated margins of the lower Ucayali basin

diet.fooditems: Strict biofilm and specialized soft algae grazer. Requires constant grazing surfaces or organic sinking supplements.
diet.feedingPlaybook: Strict biofilm and specialized soft algae grazer. Requires constant grazing surfaces or organic sinking supplements.

reproduction.comments: Adhesive egg layer on exposed plant substrates. Continuous breeder under highly pristine, mature conditions.

vibeLine.casual: The tiny striped vacuum that silently judges your algae situation.
vibeLine.pro: Otocinclus cocama. Highly sensitive to poor acclimation and requires established biofilm.

flavorText.casual: Looks adorable. Is actually a full-time employee. Will work tirelessly as long as there’s enough biofilm. Otherwise they will dramatically stare at you through the glass.
flavorText.pro: Prone to starvation in brand new tanks. Best introduced into mature, biofilm-rich systems. Excellent at controlling soft diatoms but not a replacement for proper husbandry.

ecology.comments: Highly adaptable freshwater dwarf shrimp. Appreciates leaf litter.
ecology.biotope: Slow-flowing streams and ponds in Taiwan, heavily vegetated with moss.
ecology.socialBehavior: Extremely peaceful. Appreciates being kept in groups.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Grazes constantly on biofilm and organic matter. Supplement with high-quality shrimp pellets or blanched vegetables.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Dense vegetation, mosses, and fine crevices.
reproduction.comments: Carries eggs under abdomen (berried) until they hatch into fully formed miniature shrimp.

vibeLine.casual: The tiny red janitors that make your tank feel complete.
vibeLine.pro: Neocaridina davidi. Hardy invertebrate that helps control biofilm and detritus.

flavorText.casual: Cute, busy, and surprisingly good at cleaning. They breed easily once they settle in.
flavorText.pro: Excellent cleanup crew for mature tanks. Sensitive to copper and sudden parameter changes. Best kept in groups with plenty of hiding places and biofilm.

ecology.comments: Neotenic salamander that remains aquatic throughout life. Requires cool, clean water.
ecology.biotope: Lake Xochimilco in Mexico. High altitude, cold, slow-moving water.
ecology.socialBehavior: Should be housed alone or in large tanks with same-sized axolotls. Will eat small tank mates.

diet.feedingPlaybook: Feed earthworms, bloodworms, or specialized axolotl pellets. Remove uneaten food quickly.

reproduction.layoutRequirement: Broad-leafed plants or decorations to attach eggs.
reproduction.comments: Lays hundreds of jelly-coated eggs on plants. Cannibalistic if parents are not separated.

vibeLine.casual: The walking smile that turned the hobby into a cult.
vibeLine.pro: Ambystoma mexicanum. Cool-water amphibian that requires pristine, cold water and careful handling.

flavorText.casual: Weird, wonderful, and way more popular than they have any right to be. They look like they’re always smiling at you.
flavorText.pro: Requires cool, clean, well-cycled water (16–20°C). Sensitive to poor water quality and temperature spikes. Best kept alone or with similarly sized individuals.

hiddenLore: Axolotls are famous in Minecraft for their cute colors and healing powers. Searching 'minecraft' or 'cute' reveals their hidden pixelated aura.

ecology.comments: Tiny hitchhiker snail. Reproduces rapidly. Excellent detritus cleaner. Population self-regulates with food availability. Free cleanup crew!
Nearly indestructible plant. Great for fry and shrimp.
Red/green stem plant that colors up under strong light. Easy to propagate — just trim and replant tops. Great for color contrast.
Large, colorful snail with fun personality.
Burrowing snail that aerates substrate and eats leftover food. Mostly nocturnal. Livebearing — population can boom. Great for planted tanks.
Tiny floating plant that multiplies explosively. Great nutrient absorber but nearly impossible to eradicate once introduced. You have been warned.
Smallest crypt species. Very slow-growing carpeting plant. Needs patience — takes months to fill in. No CO2 required but helps.
Bulletproof epiphyte. Do not bury rhizome.
Large rosette plant. Heavy root feeder.
Slow-growing, nearly indestructible. Attach to hardscape — never bury rhizome. Prone to algae on leaves in high light.
Floating plant with round leaves and long roots. Provides shade and cover. Absorbs excess nutrients. Keep surface movement low.
Tall, grass-like background plant. Sends out runners rapidly. Very easy — just plant and watch it spread. Dislikes liquid carbon products.
Versatile crypt in green, red, and brown varieties. May melt when transplanted but recovers from roots. Low-tech staple.
Very tall background plant with ribbon-like leaves. Can reach the surface of deep tanks. Spreads aggressively via runners.
Grows upright in flame-like pattern. Unique texture for aquascaping. Attach to vertical surfaces for best effect.
Fast-growing stem plant with lacy leaves. Changes leaf shape depending on emersed/submersed growth. Excellent nutrient sponge.
Best algae-eating snail. Won't breed in freshwater.
Triangular frond pattern resembling Christmas tree branches. Great on mesh for walls or carpets. Slightly more demanding than Java Moss.
Slow-growing rheophyte with metallic sheen on leaves. Attach to hardscape like Anubias. Many rare and collectible varieties.
Flat spiral-shelled snail in red, blue, and leopard varieties. Can reproduce quickly — some see them as pests, others love them.
Nearly transparent, inexpensive shrimp. Great cleaners and fun to observe eating. Often sold as feeder shrimp but make great pets.
Upright stem plant with rounded leaves. Very undemanding. Can grow emersed. Also used as an herbal supplement (Brahmi).
Stunning dwarf cichlid from South America.
The red nose is a great indicator of water quality.
Massive, prehistoric surface predator. Jumps out of tanks — heavy, tight lid mandatory. Only for expert fishkeepers with enormous setups.
Larger rasbora with distinctive forked tail markings. Active swimmer needing a long tank. Hardy and peaceful.
Global record found via text search fallback.
Delicate nano rainbow with thread-like fin extensions. Peaceful and shy — keep with gentle tankmates only.
Global record found via text search fallback.
